summary_text:
S:
The patient reports intermittent toe pain, redness, and stiffness for approximately two weeks, with a significant exacerbation last week during a trade show convention where he was unable to walk. He describes a throbbing pain occasionally accompanied by sharp, stabbing pain, particularly with movement. He walks on his heel to avoid bending the toe. The patient reports difficulty with the sheets at night when they come down on the affected toe. He has tried ibuprofen with limited relief. The patient denies any injury and reports no prior stomach problems.

O:
The patient's big toe exhibits a large spur and significant cartilage loss as visualized on the x-ray. There is palpable bone spurs on the dorsal aspect of the great toe joint. The patient reports tenderness to palpation on the dorsal and lateral aspects of the great toe joint, with crepitus noted upon range of motion testing. The patient's foot demonstrates limited plantar flexion (approximately 20 degrees).

A:
The patient's symptoms are consistent with hallux rigidus with significant cartilage loss and a large spur, likely causing the reported pain and stiffness. The patient's reported difficulty with the sheets at night suggests significant inflammation and discomfort.

P:
Rescribe an orthotic insert for the patient's shoe. Start meloxicam 15mg daily for pain relief. Schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to assess treatment efficacy and discuss potential interventions, including a shot or joint fusion if symptoms persist. Advise the patient to continue with ibuprofen as needed, monitoring for gastrointestinal side effects. Counsel the patient on the importance of maintaining activity levels while managing his condition and to avoid excessive walking or prolonged standing. Schedule a return precautions for worsening symptoms or lack of improvement.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right great toe pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Joseph Walker is a pleasant 58-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of right great toe pain. The onset of his pain began 2 weeks ago, however it worsened last week. He noticed the pain worsening when he was at a trade show convention and he could not ambulate as he was forced to stand there as the pain was there the whole time. He denies any specific injury. The patient describes his pain usually as throbbing and burning, but notes it occasionally changes to sharp, stabbing pain especially with movement or prolonged ambulation. His symptoms also include redness to the right great toe. The patient states that he has been ambulating on his heel to keep his toe from bending. He reports that his pain is present even with the slightest of pressure, which he notes is worse at night when his sheet is touching his right toe. He adds that he has been taking 2 ibuprofen per day, which does not provide him with relief.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient reports that he likes to bicycle ride.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right great toe pain.
Skin: Reports redness.

PHYSICAL EXAM

MSK: Examination of the right great toe reveals 10 degrees of plantar flexion with pain. Pain to palpation of the right great toe, between the big toe and 2nd toe. Palpated a bone spur on the right great toe.

RESULTS

X-ray of the right great toe taken today in office reveals a large bone spur on the anterior aspect of the right great toe joint. There is a loss of cartilage with some arthritis present.

ASSESSMENT

Right foot hallux rigidus.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his x-rays revealed hallux rigidus. We discussed treatment options for this and I have recommended that we begin with conservative treatment in the form of custom orthotics. I have also prescribed the patient meloxicam once a day to treat the pain. The patient was instructed to discontinue use and contact the office if gastrointestinal issues develop. I advised the patient that I want him to continue his regular activities.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 2 weeks to check on his progress. If his pain does not improve with the orthotics, I will recommend a cortisone injection or surgical intervention.

sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports intermittent toe pain, redness, and stiffness for approximately two weeks, with a significant exacerbation last week during a trade show convention where he was unable to walk. He describes a throbbing pain occasionally accompanied by sharp, stabbing pain, particularly with movement. He walks on his heel to avoid bending the toe. sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: The patient's big toe exhibits a large spur and significant cartilage loss as visualized on the x-ray. There is palpable bone spurs on the dorsal aspect of the great toe joint. The patient reports tenderness to palpation on the dorsal and lateral aspects of the great toe joint, with crepitus noted upon range of motion testing. sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The patient's symptoms are consistent with hallux rigidus with significant cartilage loss and a large spur, likely causing the reported pain and stiffness. The patient's reported difficulty with the sheets at night suggests significant inflammation and discomfort.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: Rescribe an orthotic insert for the patient's shoe. Start meloxicam 15mg daily for pain relief. Schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to assess treatment efficacy and discuss potential interventions, including a shot or joint fusion if symptoms persist. Advise the patient to continue with ibuprofen as needed, monitoring for gastrointestinal side effects. section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports tenderness to palpation on the dorsal and lateral aspects of the great toe joint, with crepitus noted upon range of motion testing.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This sentence mixes a patient-reported element (tenderness) with an exam finding (crepitus with ROM). Overall it functions as an objective exam description, but ideally the patient-reported component would be in S and the exam findings in O.

section_miscategorizations.text: Advise the patient to continue with ibuprofen as needed, monitoring for gastrointestinal side effects.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is correctly a plan statement (counseling on continued use and monitoring), but it implicitly references an existing medication that could also be documented in S. The primary intent is management advice, so P is acceptable though slightly mixed.
